class_schedule.topic: Network Architecture and the OSI Model
learning_objectives: Describe the seven layers of the OSI model and their functions
Compare the OSI model with the TCP/IP model
Identify how data is encapsulated at each layer
readings: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding network architecture is essential for any cybersecurity professional. We start with the foundational models that describe how networked systems communicate.
key_concepts: OSI model: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
TCP/IP model: Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application
Data encapsulation and decapsulation
Protocol Data Units (PDUs) at each layer
Network topology types: star, mesh, ring, bus
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Wireshark to observe packet encapsulation and identify OSI layers in captured traffic

review.value: Seven-layer reference model for network communication; each layer provides services to the layer above and receives services from the layer below
Process of wrapping data with protocol headers at each layer: Application data becomes segments, packets, frames, and bits

learning_objectives: Calculate subnet addresses, broadcast addresses, and host ranges
Apply CIDR notation and VLSM concepts
Design an IP addressing scheme for a small network
readings: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: IP addressing is the addressing system of the internet. Mastering subnetting is a non-negotiable skill for network and security professionals.
key_concepts: IPv4 address classes and private ranges
Subnet masks and CIDR notation
Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM)
IPv6 addressing and transition mechanisms
Network Address Translation (NAT) and its security implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Subnetting a Class C network into multiple subnets and verifying connectivity in GNS3

review.value: Classless Inter-Domain Routing uses slash notation (e.g., /24) to specify the number of network bits, replacing rigid class-based addressing
NAT obscures internal IP addresses from external networks, providing a basic layer of obscurity but not true security

learning_objectives: Analyze TCP three-way handshake and connection teardown
Differentiate between TCP and UDP use cases
Identify common ports and their associated services

lecture_notes.introduction: TCP and UDP are the workhorses of internet communication. Understanding their behavior is critical for analyzing network traffic and identifying anomalies.
key_concepts: TCP three-way handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
TCP connection teardown: FIN, FIN-ACK
TCP vs UDP: reliability vs speed tradeoffs
Common ports: 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 53 (DNS), 25 (SMTP)
TCP flags and their role in scanning techniques
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capturing and analyzing a TCP handshake in Wireshark, then comparing with UDP DNS traffic
setup_requirements: Web browser for generating HTTP traffic
nslookup or dig for DNS queries

review.value: Three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection: Client sends SYN, Server responds SYN-ACK, Client confirms with ACK
TCP provides ordered, reliable delivery with flow control; UDP provides fast, connectionless delivery without guarantees

learning_objectives: Explain how routers make forwarding decisions
Compare static and dynamic routing protocols
Understand Layer 2 switching and MAC address tables
readings: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach Ch. 5
Cisco CCNA Routing and Switching Guide Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Routers and switches are the backbone of network infrastructure. Understanding how they operate is essential for both network design and security analysis.
key_concepts: Routing tables and forwarding decisions
Static vs dynamic routing (OSPF, BGP, EIGRP)
Layer 2 switching and MAC address tables
ARP protocol and ARP spoofing risks
Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) and loop prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring static routes and OSPF on virtual routers in GNS3, observing routing table convergence

review.value: Protocols like OSPF use algorithms to automatically discover and maintain optimal paths, adapting to network topology changes
Attacker sends forged ARP replies to associate their MAC address with a legitimate IP, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks

class_schedule.topic: DNS, DHCP, and Network Services Security
learning_objectives: Analyze DNS resolution process and identify attack vectors
Explain DHCP operations and starvation attacks
Implement DNSSEC concepts for DNS security
readings: NIST SP 800-81 Secure DNS Deployment Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Network services like DNS and DHCP are fundamental to network operations but present significant attack surfaces if not properly secured.
key_concepts: DNS hierarchy: root servers, TLD, authoritative, recursive resolvers
DNS attacks: cache poisoning, DNS tunneling, amplification
DNSSEC: signing zones and validating responses
DHCP operation: DORA process
DHCP attacks: starvation, rogue servers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a BIND DNS server, performing DNS cache poisoning in the lab, and implementing DNSSEC
setup_requirements: BIND9 DNS server on Linux VM

review.value: Attacker injects forged DNS responses into a resolver's cache, redirecting users to malicious sites
Attacker floods the DHCP server with requests using spoofed MAC addresses, exhausting the available IP pool

learning_objectives: Compare wireless security protocols (WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3)

lecture_notes.introduction: Wireless networks extend the attack surface beyond physical boundaries. Understanding wireless security is critical in today's mobile-first environment.
key_concepts: 802.11 standards and frequency bands
WEP vulnerabilities and why it must never be used
WPA2-Enterprise with RADIUS authentication
WPA3 and Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE)
Wireless attacks: evil twin, deauthentication, KRACK
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capturing a WPA2 four-way handshake with airodump-ng and demonstrating why strong passphrases matter

review.value: WPA3 replaces PSK with SAE (Dragonfly handshake), providing forward secrecy and resistance to offline dictionary attacks
Attacker creates a rogue access point mimicking a legitimate one, intercepting traffic from clients that connect to it

quizzes.topic: OSI Model, IP Addressing, and TCP/IP

questions.question: Given the IP address 192.168.10.0/26, calculate: (a) the number of usable host addresses per subnet, (b) the subnet mask in dotted decimal, and (c) the broadcast address of the first subnet.
questions.answer: (a) 62 usable hosts (2^6 - 2 = 62). (b) 255.255.255.192. (c) 192.168.10.63 (network 192.168.10.0, broadcast is the last address before the next subnet at 192.168.10.64).

rubric.key: Correct host count with calculation shown

questions.question: During a TCP three-way handshake, explain what happens if the server receives a SYN packet but the client never sends the final ACK. What type of attack exploits this, and how is it mitigated?
questions.answer: The server allocates resources for a half-open connection and waits for the ACK. This is a SYN flood attack, where an attacker sends many SYN packets without completing the handshake, exhausting the server's connection table. Mitigation includes SYN cookies (server encodes connection state in the SYN-ACK sequence number, avoiding resource allocation until ACK is received), rate limiting, and firewall-based SYN proxy.

rubric.key: Explains SYN cookies or other valid mitigation

quizzes.topic: Routing, Network Services, and Wireless Security

questions.question: Explain how a DNS cache poisoning attack works at the protocol level and describe two specific defenses against it.
questions.answer: The attacker sends forged DNS responses to a recursive resolver, matching the transaction ID and arriving before the legitimate response. The resolver caches the forged record, redirecting future queries to a malicious IP. Defenses: (1) DNSSEC validates responses using digital signatures chained to a trust anchor, ensuring authenticity and integrity. (2) Source port randomization makes it exponentially harder for attackers to guess the correct transaction ID and port combination.

questions.question: A company's WPA2-PSK wireless network was compromised. The attacker captured the four-way handshake and performed an offline dictionary attack. What specific weakness in WPA2-PSK enabled this, and how would migrating to WPA3-SAE prevent it?
questions.answer: WPA2-PSK derives the Pairwise Master Key directly from the passphrase and SSID, so capturing the four-way handshake allows offline brute-force attempts against the passphrase without further network interaction. WPA3-SAE uses the Dragonfly key exchange (a Password-Authenticated Key Exchange protocol), where each handshake attempt requires an active interaction with the access point. This eliminates offline dictionary attacks and provides forward secrecy, so even if the passphrase is later compromised, previously captured sessions remain encrypted.

major_assignments.title: Network Analysis and Security Audit
major_assignments.description: Capture, analyze, and document network traffic from a provided lab environment. Identify all active hosts, services, protocols, and potential security issues. Produce a comprehensive network audit report.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency with packet capture and analysis tools
Identify network protocols and their security implications
Map network topology from captured traffic
Produce a professional network audit report
deliverables: Network topology diagram with all discovered hosts and services
Protocol analysis report documenting all identified traffic patterns
Security findings with risk ratings and remediation steps

criteria: All significant traffic patterns identified
Correct protocol identification at each OSI layer
Accurate flow analysis and session reconstruction
Proper use of display and capture filters
Complete host discovery with IP and MAC addresses
All running services identified with port numbers
Accurate topology diagram with correct interconnections
VLAN and subnet identification
Unencrypted traffic identified and flagged
Protocol vulnerabilities documented
Anomalous traffic patterns detected
Actionable remediation recommendations
Professional report formatting
Clear technical writing
Screenshots and evidence properly labeled
Methodology section included
Thorough and accurate traffic analysis with all protocols identified
Complete network map with all hosts, services, and interconnections
Insightful security findings with practical remediation plan
Professional documentation suitable for a real audit engagement

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of networking concepts and protocol knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises with Wireshark, GNS3, and network configuration tools

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive network analysis and security audit project

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering OSI model, TCP/IP, routing, and subnetting

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all networking and network security topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. Packet capture analysis and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted captures must be from the designated lab environment. Fabricating or modifying pcap files is considered academic dishonesty.
course_policies.lab_safety: Network scanning and traffic capture must only be performed within the isolated lab environment. Unauthorized scanning of production networks will result in course failure.
